item.title: Supermarkets struggle to maintain sugar supplies
item.description: Empty shelves and dwindling supplies of sugar are causing concern among retailers and consumers across parts of Viti Levu, with supermarkets struggling to secure regular deliveries. Maxvalue purchasing officer Sonia Prakash said the supermarket’s warehouse had run out of sugar, with the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) advising of production issues affecting supply. She said FSC had only 25kg retail p
item.content:encoded:
Empty shelves and dwindling supplies of sugar are causing concern among retailers and consumers across parts of Viti Levu, with supermarkets struggling to secure regular deliveries.

Maxvalue purchasing officer Sonia Prakash said the supermarket’s warehouse had run out of sugar, with the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) advising of production issues affecting supply. She said FSC had only 25kg retail packs available when contacted.

RB Patel’s Lami branch has also been affected, with manager Sumal Mohammed confirming shelves had been empty since last week.

To manage limited stocks, the supermarket is restricting purchases to 2kg per customer to prevent bulk buying.

Consumers in Navua have also reported sugar shortages.

Attempts to obtain comments from the Sugar Minister were unsuccessful when this edition went to press.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Green light to import sugar
item.description: BUSINESSES have been given the green light by the Government to import sugar from overseas markets. In an interview in Nadi yesterday Minister of Finance Esrom Immanuel said although there is some sufficient stock in supermarkets, Government has allowed businesses to import sugar.
item.content:encoded:
BUSINESSES have been given the green light by the Government to import sugar from overseas markets.

In an interview in Nadi yesterday Minister of Finance Esrom Immanuel said although there is some sufficient stock in supermarkets, Government has allowed businesses to import sugar.

This follows concerns that some supermarkets across the country had little to no sugar on their shelves.

“We had checked last week. The Ministry of Agriculture and Sugar Industry had mentioned there is some sufficient stock,” Mr Immanuel said.

“But we have allowed businesses, if they can import sugar as well because it will cater for the expected shortage.

“However, on the bright side the 2026 harvesting season has already started and that probably can cater for sugar supply in the next two to three months but for now we have allowed any importer to also import sugar if there is any shortage.”

Meanwhile, Minister for Multi-Ethnic Affairs and Labasa cane farmer Charan Jeath Singh said the country may import sugar from Thailand if farmers were not careful.

“It’s much cheaper to import and sell than what it costs to produce sugar locally,” he said.

“Let’s not kill this industry, don’t listen to bystanders of the industry, let’s make it work.

“Sugar industry is very competitive in the global market. It needs economics of scale to survive.”

Fiji primarily imports sugar from Indonesia, New Zealand, Australia, Malaysia, and Thailand to meet domestic consumer demands and supplement supply when local production falls short of consumption needs.

Attempts to get comments from Fiji Sugar Corporation chief executive officer Bhan Pratap Singh were unsuccessful as he declined the calls made to him yesterday.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Strategy to guide future peacekeeping engagements
item.description: FIJI is preparing its first National Peace Operations Strategy to strengthen and guide the country’s future involvement in international peacekeeping missions. Acting Attorney-General and Minister for Justice Siromi Turaga said the strategy recognised the changing nature of global security challenges, where the boundaries between traditional and non-traditional threats were becoming increasingly u
item.content:encoded:
FIJI is preparing its first National Peace Operations Strategy to strengthen and guide the country’s future involvement in international peacekeeping missions.

Acting Attorney-General and Minister for Justice Siromi Turaga said the strategy recognised the changing nature of global security challenges, where the boundaries between traditional and non-traditional threats were becoming increasingly unclear.

He said the national security strategy had highlighted these emerging challenges, with the new peace operations framework expected to shape the country’s future contributions to global peace efforts.

“Since the first deployment to Lebanon in 1978, more than 15,000 Fijians have served in peacekeeping missions across the world, often in some of the world’s most difficult and dangerous environments,” he said.

He said more than 65 Fijians had lost their lives while serving overseas, reflecting the sacrifices made by those involved in peace operations.

Fiji’s peacekeeping role has also expanded beyond the military, with police officers and corrections personnel contributing to international missions.

Mr Turaga said protecting civilians, especially women and children, remained a key priority for peacekeepers, guided by the principle of human dignity.

While acknowledging pressures on global peacekeeping budgets, he said Fiji would continue deploying personnel when needed.

“Our goal is not simply the absence of conflict, but the creation of conditions where peace can thrive, institutions can endure and future generations can prosper.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Scams cost Fiji $1.3m
item.description: SCAMS have cost Fijians more than $1.3million since 2017, with 1672 cases recorded nationwide, but only 338 have been prosecuted. Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu revealed the figures yesterday at the launch of Anti-Scam Awareness Week, saying the remaining cases reported since 2017 were still under investigation.
item.content:encoded:
SCAMS have cost Fijians more than $1.3million since 2017, with 1672 cases recorded nationwide, but only 338 have been prosecuted.

Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu revealed the figures yesterday at the launch of Anti-Scam Awareness Week, saying the remaining cases reported since 2017 were still under investigation.

He said the Western Division recorded the highest number of scam reports, followed by the Southern, Central, Eastern and Northern divisions.

“Cases still under investigation in the Western Division alone involve more than $500,000,” he said.

“Over the last seven years, 11 cases were prosecuted with the accused persons receiving varying convictions from imprisonment terms, ordered to repay the victims, or issued a fine.

“Two cases were acquitted as the victims failed to turn up to court despite being summoned.”

Mr Tudravu said the Fiji Police Force had strengthened its response through the Anti-Scams Taskforce by improving coordination and streamlining reporting processes.

He said a dedicated Scams Section had also been established, comprising specialised investigators with digital literacy and forensic expertise to investigate cyber-enabled scam syndicates.

“To defeat a decentralised, highly sophisticated, and borderless threat, our traditional concepts of policing must adapt.”

He said the specialised unit reflected the force’s commitment to tackling the growing threat of online and cyber-enabled scams.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Citizenship applications top 5700
item.description: The Department of Immigration received 5795 citizenship applications between 2019 and 2023, processing more than 90 per cent of them while declining just 23. The figures were provided to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence and tabled in Parliament last Friday.
item.content:encoded:
The Department of Immigration received 5795 citizenship applications between 2019 and 2023, processing more than 90 per cent of them while declining just 23.

The figures were provided to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence and tabled in Parliament last Friday.

According to the report, the department processed 5385 citizenship applications during the five-year period, closely matching the number of applications received.

The highest number of applications was recorded in 2022, when 1586 applications were lodged, followed by 1248 in 2019, 1161 in 2020, 751 in 2021 and 1049 in 2023.

Applications involving minors also remained consistently high across all five years, while former citizenship applications accounted for another significant share of the department’s workload.

The report notes that spouse and naturalisation applications fluctuated over the reporting period, with noticeably fewer applications during 2021 and 2022, while late registration applications remained comparatively low and stable.

Only 23 citizenship applications were declined over the five years, with no applications rejected in 2019, six declined in 2020, seven in 2021, eight in 2022 and two in 2023.

Immigration targets work permit breaches

Breaches of work permit conditions and the exploitation of foreign workers through overseas recruitment agencies remain among the most common immigration offences in Fiji, according to the Department of Immigration.

The information was provided to the Standing Committee on Foreign Affairs and Defence and tabled in Parliament last Friday.

The department said many offences occur when permit holders fail to comply with the terms and conditions attached to their permits, which are designed to protect the local labour market and ensure fair employment practices.

According to the report, a common breach involves foreign workers taking up employment with a different employer or performing duties outside those approved under their work permits without proper authorisation.

The department said work permits are issued for a specific employer and role, and any unauthorised change in employment or job responsibilities constitutes an immigration offence.

It also highlighted cases where permit holders work in sectors where qualified local workers are available, saying such practices undermine employment opportunities for Fijians.

“Compliance with these conditions is essential to maintain the integrity of the immigration system and to ensure that opportunities for local workers are safeguarded,” the report states.

The department also identified a growing trend involving the use of overseas recruitment agencies by local employers to source foreign workers.

According to the report, some workers are required to sign multiple employment contracts — one in their home country and another after arriving in Fiji.

The department said workers were often initially offered attractive salaries and employment conditions overseas, only to be presented with a second contract in Fiji that contains significantly lower pay and less favourable conditions.

The report identifies the practice as an emerging concern for immigration authorities, highlighting the need for stronger oversight of overseas recruitment arrangements and compliance with work permit conditions.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: The walk for hope
item.description: A thought that began during a workout has grown into a 226-kilometre journey carrying a message of hope, resilience and change. Fijian soldiers serving in the British Army are trekking across Viti Levu with 25-kilogram packs on their backs to raise awareness about the dangers of drug abuse while supporting veterans in Fiji and the UK.
item.content:encoded:
A thought that began during a workout has grown into a 226-kilometre journey carrying a message of hope, resilience and change.

Fijian soldiers serving in the British Army are trekking across Viti Levu with 25-kilogram packs on their backs to raise awareness about the dangers of drug abuse while supporting veterans in Fiji and the UK.

The nine-day charity walk, which began in Lautoka on July 14, will conclude in Tailevu tomorrow.

Soldiers in the team include Sergeant Autiko Domonakibau, Sergeant Filipe Buatavatava, Guardsman Timilai Navakacagi and Guardsman Viliame Vakavera.

The fundraiser is supporting the Royal British Legion, with funds to be shared between its Fiji and UK branches.

Sgt Buatavatava said the greater purpose behind the challenge was to encourage young Fijians to reject drugs and choose a positive future.

The soldiers have visited schools along the Coral Coast during their walk, speaking to students about how drugs affects individuals, families and communities.

Sgt Buatavatava said the idea came unexpectedly while he was reflecting on his 18 years of military service. He hopes the miles covered will inspire young people to make better choices and build a future free from the harm caused by drugs.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Officer’s death probe delayed
item.description: THE investigation into the death of Police Constable Peniasi Racagi is being delayed because Fiji lacks the forensic equipment needed to conduct advanced post-mortem analysis locally. Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu yesterday said the shortage has forced authorities to send post-mortem samples and specimens to laboratories in New Zealand, extending the time needed to progress investigations.
item.content:encoded:
THE investigation into the death of Police Constable Peniasi Racagi is being delayed because Fiji lacks the forensic equipment needed to conduct advanced post-mortem analysis locally.

Police Commissioner Rusiate Tudravu yesterday said the shortage has forced authorities to send post-mortem samples and specimens to laboratories in New Zealand, extending the time needed to progress investigations.

Mr Tudravu said police were waiting for the results of forensic testing from New Zealand before they could move forward with the case.

“We need the equipments in order for us to do our job well,” Mr Tudravu said.

“If we cannot be provided that, then all these things will be prolonged.”

He said Fiji would continue relying on overseas laboratories until the necessary forensic equipment was available locally.

Mr Tudravu said the expected turnaround time for the results was three weeks, although the process could take longer if other issues arose.

“I plead and urge the relatives, the victims to bear with us on that. It is out of our control and we are also waiting.”

Constable Racagi, who was assigned to the Lami Police Station, went missing during night shift on February 20 and his body was later discovered floating near the beach behind the Lami Oceania Wesley Church the next morning.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Man cops two-year prison sentence for defrauding State
item.description: A MAN has been handed a two-year prison sentence after being convicted of conspiring with his former wife to defraud the Department of Heritage and Arts out of $660 using forged vehicle hire receipts. Court records show Nick Stark, a former employee with the RPA Group, conspired with his former wife, Rosalia Muavesi, who was then an employee of the Department of Heritage and Arts, to obtain a gain
item.content:encoded:
A MAN has been handed a two-year prison sentence after being convicted of conspiring with his former wife to defraud the Department of Heritage and Arts out of $660 using forged vehicle hire receipts.

Court records show Nick Stark, a former employee with the RPA Group, conspired with his former wife, Rosalia Muavesi, who was then an employee of the Department of Heritage and Arts, to obtain a gain from the department dishonestly.

It was further established that Stark aided and abetted Muavesi in obtaining the money and personally received $220 from the proceeds.

Passing sentence on July 15, Magistrate Shageeth Somaratne ordered Stark’s head sentence be partly suspended, directing that he serve three months in immediate custody.

Since Stark had already spent three months in remand, the court considered this period as time served and suspended the remaining 21 months of the sentence for three years.

Mr Somaratne said a Stark has also been ordered to pay a $1000 fine or serve 100 days in jail in default of payment.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
